title: Syntax and Grammar Core
description: Clause structure, constituency, grammatical functions, subordination, coordination, complementation, and information structure.
upgradePremise: Expanded for Nyx and Marion with stronger grammatical reasoning, case-based analysis, modules, projects, and explicit source coverage.

forNyx: Prefer precise, grammatical phrasing that sounds natural rather than stiff.
Use register-sensitive wording so conversational output and formal output do not collapse into one style.
When uncertain, preserve meaning first, then improve form through revision rather than over-correcting.
forMarion: Use the pack to explain why a wording choice works, not just whether it is 'right' or 'wrong'.
Ground revision advice in form-function reasoning, register, audience, and evidence.
Support coaching, diagnostics, and portfolio-style improvement tracking.

topics.name: Constituency and Functions
coreClaims: Constituents form hierarchical structures (phrases inside clauses).
Grammatical functions (subject/object/complement/adjunct) differ from word class labels.
practice: Bracket a sentence into NP/VP/PP units.
Explain function-role: 'NP' can be subject or object depending on position/structure.

topics.name: Clause Types and Subordination
coreClaims: Independent vs dependent clauses behave differently with respect to tense, embedding, and linkage.
Subordinate clauses can act as complements or adjuncts.
practice: Classify subordinate clause role (complement vs adjunct).
Diagnose common learner errors: fragment vs stylistic non-finite clause.

topics.name: Coordination and Parallelism
coreClaims: Coordination joins comparable structures; mismatched coordination often creates processing difficulty.
Parallelism is both a grammatical and rhetorical constraint.
practice: Repair parallelism errors without changing meaning.
Explain why a coordination is unbalanced (category/function mismatch).

topics.name: Complementation
coreClaims: Verbs and adjectives license specific complement patterns (that-clauses, infinitives, -ing clauses).
Choice of complement often shifts meaning (promise vs persuade patterns).
practice: Write a brief usage note: what patterns are acceptable in academic register.

modules.name: Constituency and Function
outcomes: Bracket clauses into plausible phrase units.
Separate form labels from syntactic functions.

modules.name: Complement Frames
outcomes: Match verbs and adjectives to acceptable complement patterns.
Notice meaning shifts across complement choices.

modules.name: Parallelism and Processing
outcomes: Explain why parallelism aids readability and rhetoric.

caseStudies.title: Nyx Produces a Grammatical Tangle
caseStudies.scenario: The sentence is locally correct but globally hard to parse because embedding, coordination, or complement choice becomes overloaded.
intervention: Prefer explicit subordination when ambiguity rises.

caseStudies.title: Marion Gives Accurate Advice but Uses Fragile Sentence Frames
caseStudies.scenario: The explanation depends on unstable clause chaining that increases misread risk.
intervention: Expose the logical relation with overt connectives.
Test with substitution or movement diagnostics where helpful.

projects.name: Sentence Frame Library
projects.goal: Build reliable sentence templates for explanation, caution, contrast, evidence, and concession across registers.

referenceTextsUsed.citationAPA: Quirk, R., Greenbaum, S., Leech, G., & Svartvik, J. (1985). A comprehensive grammar of the English language. Longman.

referenceTextsUsed.title: Hit Parade of Errors in Grammar, Punctuation, and Style

normalizationMeta.lineAuditPass: full-file structural and runtime retrieval audit

keywords: explain why a coordination is unbalanced
match verbs and adjectives to acceptable
what patterns are acceptable in academic

auditSummary.notes: Normalized for consistent Marion retrieval, alias routing, and source coverage tracking.

definitionTerms.definition: Syntax is the study of how words and phrases combine into clauses and sentences.
Grammar is the system of patterns that organizes words, phrases, clauses, and meanings in a language.
A clause is a grammatical unit built around a predicate, usually with a subject and a verb phrase.
Constituency is the hierarchical grouping of words into phrases and larger structures.
Parallelism is the use of matching grammatical structures to make coordinated ideas easier to process.
Complementation is the pattern by which verbs, adjectives, or nouns require or allow completing elements such as clauses, objects, or infinitives.

runtimeDefinitionIndex.fallbackRule: If term appears here, answer directly. Do not ask for more detail unless the user requests a specific register, example, or comparison.

rules: If the user asks 'what is', 'define', 'meaning of', or 'explain' plus a known English term, answer directly from this domain before generic fallback.
Do not route English definition questions into technical_debug unless the user explicitly asks for backend, code, file, audit, autopsy, patch, route, script, endpoint, deployment, or validation.
If a term overlaps with another domain, give a brief neutral definition, name the possible English interpretation, and ask one targeted clarification question.
Preserve meaning first; revise form second; never over-correct when the user asks for explanation rather than rewriting.
No user-facing generic fallback such as 'I need one more concrete detail' when the term is present in this pack. Use answer + clarification instead.
blockedFallbacks: I need one more concrete detail to route this cleanly.
I’ll keep this broad and practical
